content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural grade reorganization (lower grades stay, upper grades centralize)'. Closing small-village primary schools in depopulating Hungarian settlements is not justified as an efficiency measure on current evidence, because per-pupil cost premiums are economically defensible, learning outcomes do not suffer materially, and closure-induced local population loss and community harm systematically outweigh accounting savings.
opportunities: Enables full-time specialist subject teaching at the centralized upper-grade site, addressing the documented Polish problem of rural teachers commuting between multiple part-time postings.

Preserves a local school presence for the youngest, most travel-vulnerable children (grades 1-4/1-6), partially mitigating the documented burden of early-morning long-distance travel for young pupils.
strengths: Hungary's primary education school-site network declined from 3,609 sites (2021) to 3,521 sites (2024), while over 1,200 of 3,155 settlements now have no public educational institution.
Hungary's annual births fell 22.5% in ten years (110,800 in 2015 to 77,600 in 2025), with primary-grade enrollment projected to shrink from ~110,000 to ~72,000 annually by 2030.
threats: Poland's 1999 reform was politically reversible and was in fact reversed after 17 years; without concrete cross-party entrenchment mechanisms or constitutional-level protection, Hungary's version faces similar reversal risk, especially given the documented absence of any cross-party consensus mechanism for rural school reform.

Teacher redeployment under Act LII of 2023 (Status Law), which grants district centers authority to reassign teachers, may trigger the kind of mobilization seen in the Tanítanék movement if perceived as involuntary displacement without adequate consultation.
weaknesses: The magnitude and timeline of actual primary-grade enrollment loss in rural versus urban areas remain uncertain; district-level projections are scarce.

The actual cost differential between small-village school operation and consolidated/bussed alternatives (including all transportation, facilities, teacher deployment, and psychosocial costs) has not been reliably estimated for Hungary.
